skill.name: azure-storage-queue-ts
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Queue Storage JavaScript and TypeScript SDK. Covers authentication, queue operations, message processing, encoding, SAS tokens, errors, and platform limits.
skill.summary: Build Azure Queue Storage workflows with the TypeScript SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The critical heuristic findings are dismissed because the file is SDK documentation, not executable skill code. Static command-execution alerts come from Markdown fences and TypeScript template literals. The remaining risk is legitimate but meaningful: examples read Azure credentials from environment variables, create SAS tokens, connect to Azure endpoints, and show destructive queue operations.

medium_findings.title: Credential and SAS Token Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill shows examples that read Azure account names, account keys, connection strings, and SAS tokens from environment variables. This is normal for Azure SDK documentation, but users must avoid exposing these values in prompts, logs, browser clients, or generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly use environment-backed Azure credentials and generate SAS tokens. The context is legitimate SDK guidance, so the issue is operational sensitivity rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Queue Operation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for deleting queues, deleting messages, and clearing all messages. These are expected Queue Storage operations, but generated code can cause data loss if used against production queues without safeguards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples call destructive Azure Queue Storage APIs. They are appropriate for this SDK skill, but they create a real misuse risk.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many Markdown code fences and TypeScript template literals as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed file is documentation with fenced examples and does not define a runnable script that executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Markdown fences or TypeScript snippets, not Ruby backtick execution. No child_process, eval, spawn, curl, or wget behavior was found in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint URLs Are Expected Service Targets
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL alerts point to Azure Queue Storage endpoint templates built from an account name. These are normal SDK endpoint examples, not arbitrary exfiltration destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed URL examples target the standard queue.core.windows.net Azure Queue Storage domain. No unrelated external service or credential-posting endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decode Is Message Encoding Guidance
low_findings.description: The base64 pattern is part of a custom Queue Storage message encoder and decoder example. It is not used to hide code, load a payload, or bypass review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The encoding and decoding functions operate on queue message text inside an SDK example. There is no subsequent execution or dynamic loading of decoded content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Unsupported
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the frontmatter description line. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, custom encryption, or weak cryptographic API usage was found in that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 begins the YAML description block and does not contain cryptographic code. The reviewed context describes Azure Queue Storage message operations.

content.user_title: Manage Azure Queue Storage in TypeScript
content.value_statement: Azure queue code often requires careful SDK setup and message handling. This skill provides TypeScript patterns for authentication, queue operations, workers, encoding, and SAS tokens.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import @azure/storage-queue with @azure/identity.
Provides authentication patterns for DefaultAzureCredential, connection strings, shared keys, and SAS tokens.
Covers queue creation, listing, metadata, deletion, and properties.
Explains sending, receiving, peeking, updating, deleting, and clearing messages.
Includes worker, poison message, visibility extension, and encoding patterns.
Summarizes SDK limits, error handling, and Node.js versus browser support.
limitations: It is reference guidance, not an executable tool or Azure account integration.
It does not validate live Azure credentials, queues, or permissions.
It does not replace current Azure documentation for version-specific changes.
It includes destructive operation examples that require production safeguards.

use_cases.title: Create queue producers
use_cases.description: Generate TypeScript examples for sending plain text, delayed messages, and structured payloads to Azure Queue Storage.

use_cases.title: Design queue workers
use_cases.description: Plan message receive loops with visibility timeouts, deletion after processing, retries, and poison queue handling.

use_cases.title: Review queue security
use_cases.description: Compare credential options, SAS token usage, and browser versus Node.js support before implementing queue access.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic sender
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me a TypeScript example that creates a QueueClient and sends one message using DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Build a worker loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript worker pattern for receiving up to 10 Azure Queue Storage messages and deleting each one after successful processing.

prompt_templates.title: Handle poison messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a poison message strategy for Azure Queue Storage with a maximum dequeue count and a separate poison queue.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate secure access
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare DefaultAzureCredential, connection strings, shared keys, and SAS tokens for a Node.js Azure Queue Storage service.

output_examples.input: I need a queue sender for a backend service.
output: Client setup: QueueServiceClient with the account queue endpoint.
Message flow: get the queue client, create the queue if needed, then send a string payload.
Use a visibility timeout that matches normal processing time.
Extend visibility before the timeout expires for long tasks.
Delete the message only after processing succeeds.

output_examples.input: I need temporary queue access for another service.
output: Use the narrowest SAS permissions possible.
Avoid exposing SAS tokens in browser logs, prompts, or committed files.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential over account keys and connection strings when the runtime supports it.
Set visibility timeouts based on expected processing time and extend them for long-running work.
Move repeatedly failing messages to a poison queue after a clear retry threshold.
anti_patterns: Do not paste account keys, connection strings, or SAS tokens into prompts or source control.
Do not clear or delete production queues without an explicit environment check.
Do not grant broad account SAS permissions when queue-level permissions are enough.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my Azure account?
faq.answer: No. It provides code guidance and examples only.

faq.question: Which authentication method should I prefer?
faq.answer: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential when possible because it avoids storing account keys in application configuration.

faq.question: Can this help with poison messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a pattern for checking dequeue count and moving failed messages to a poison queue.

faq.question: Does Azure Queue Storage store objects directly?
faq.answer: No. Messages must be strings, so structured data should be serialized before sending.

faq.question: Can I use all features in a browser?
faq.answer: No. Shared key credentials, SAS generation, and DefaultAzureCredential are listed as Node.js only.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It teaches credential, SAS token, network endpoint, and destructive queue operation patterns that require careful handling.
